speaker: interviewee
text: So will be four tapes, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first encounter a Commandant Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just when we arrive in the concentration camp Krakau-Plaszow, we saw him screaming, yelling. The dogs, lots of dogs, were barking. And this is, just to see him was scary, you know, because we knew who he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: People been talking. People will been talking. And they put us in barracks. They been barracks with bunks, three-- one, two, three. And together with my sister, we were in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were women and men separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, separated. And first, I worked with the brush factory. And I saw this killing. And then they moved me to the wash place when the laundry, I was washing the bloody sheets.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bloody sheets from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Ghetto. If they brought these up to Plaszow from Ghetto, because Ghetto was liquidated. Was no more Ghetto. They brought the bloody sheets to the laundry place. And we were washing like this, by hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why the sheets were bloody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they bloody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they been shooting people in the Ghetto. So and blood come very hard out. So we have stoves there and big container with water and soap. And we have to boil this. And when I was not longer there, come a little Jewish man, and he asked for 10 girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you about this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was '40-- 20. I was 20 year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me about that, can you tell me more about Commandant Goeth, what you saw him do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He hang my friend. He wasn't my friend, he was much older. But I knew the people. He in very rich in Krakau there, a big store with leather, but very good people, very intelligent people. But they knew he has lots of money, but not in concentration camp. Already they took everything from him, and they hang him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Goeth hanged him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not him, but he let him to hang.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hanged him. They have a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In front of the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have to see this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you why they were hanging him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't say. They didn't need explanation. They did what they want to do. And poor man, he was a good, very religious man, good Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did Goeth do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he left behind his wife and his daughter. They been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in the camp with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other encounters or times did you see Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was going on the horse, riding horse. Sometime we see him, but not very often. It was very scary to see him, especially we hear barking the dogs. This was so scary. We didn't know what is going on. And they are start to be the uppers. Early in morning, they been blowing on the-- how you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whistles?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the whistles. Everybody has to go out in winter, in summer, and stand in lines. And they've been counting us, hours and hours. Sometime was so terrible cold or so, so very hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why they called you out to count you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They call upper, because if somebody didn't run away so they count. Nonsense, you know, just to make hard the life, this what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the prisoners talk about Goeth a lot among yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody was scared. He was a very scary person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What stories did you hear about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: For example, he has a girl, she worked for him. And he beat her daily. And a guy, he worked for him. Something was not clean enough what he wanted, he shoot him. I think he was 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they work for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his villa. He has a villa, in the beginning. How we work in the camp. In the beginning, he has a villa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else about Goeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that he was a monster. This was not a human being. And he make, Schindler, he make connection with him. And he party with him. And so was possibility that he let us go to Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. You said a Jewish man came to select 10 girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he buy a factory. I think this was one time a Jewish factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who bought the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler, Oskar Schindler. And he owned the factory. And he need people for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: An armor factory. Pots and pans we did there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the pots and pans being made for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for serving. It was like business. They sell to stores. And one group was airport was men, so they came, work for Schindler. And the girls who he select, the guy, we work for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who selected?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy who took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the man who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Jewish little guy. And he came to the laundry place. And he chose 10 girls. I was among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe him? Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know his name. I cannot remember his face, only that he was, he was a little man. And I was washing the laundry, and he say, come with me. I didn't know first what is going to be, where I am going then. Put us on a truck and took us, it's called Zablocie. It was outskirt of Krakau. There he has new factory and was a big sign, Emalja. If you saw the movies, there the the emalja.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Is E-M-A-L-J-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emalja, this means that the enamel, is in Polish emalja. The enamel pots and pans was made there. And was the owner was was Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: The owner was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see or hear about Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked in the factory, they put me there. Right away, we work. We live not so far from the factory. We have to walk back and forth. He built for us barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who built barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler, he built the barracks for us, for men and for woman. Was much better than in Plaszow, all the condition was better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions, how it was better?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was better. The coffee was better. And it was nicer, much nicer. It was not three the banks, was only two. We don't have to go so high and more comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: These belong to Oskar Schindler, he builded this. And this belong to him. It was for all men. And there I got this tattoo [INAUDIBLE] in the skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is abbreviation of German word konzentrationslager. Is like concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say? What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have this?

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Konzentrationslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that a-- what are those letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: KL.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did this mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: This means concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did it signify, that you had KL on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose was that we not run away. That we are marked but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this mean that you worked for someone in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this means that I belong to a camp as a prisoner. And I am a prisoner. If I run away, somebody see this, so they shoot me right away, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they put this on your arm in the barracks for Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but this was not that Schindler wanted, because he has his bosses also, you know-- SS and the German Nazi. They did what they wanted. So they say, you have here people is outside of the concentration camp, is a little concentration. They can run away. So they put this on us. Everyone who was at Schindler has this.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the Schindler's List Jews have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: --KL?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me when you first met Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work in his factory, so he make rounds every day. And he make a great impression on me. Here is the picture somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll see it at the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tall, handsome man, always cigarette in his hands, lots of good perfume. The whole factory was smelling if he come in, every day he came. He has a big diamond on his finger that was shining, the diamond. And always smile on his face, always a young girl on his side. He never came by himself. He wasn't very faithful to his wife. But I am not criticize him, because he was good to us. But what is the truth, I say. And he make rounds and very, always smiling, always nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever speak with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one time I speak with him. And I ask him whether he can bring to his factory my father and my sister. And he not promise me. He say, it's not in his power to do this, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he talk to the prisoners when he would come into the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he will talk to prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the prisoners say about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody likes him, everybody. But if came somebody like him, all the bosses, I told you, we make the sign six, that we are careful, that somebody is coming in factory and we have work. Otherwise, if we didn't work a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that to me in more detail, the sign, what the prisoners did?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's six, sign is six. If they show this, nobody can know what this means, but we knew it, that it mean it come somebody to check-- whether it was Schindler, or whether was another German, or something, or somebody for checking us, they make six. And then we stay by our work and start to work. Otherwise, we didn't work a lot. We weren't very productive in his factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We [INAUDIBLE] the pots and pans. We put this in a solution where it was the enamel. Then we put this on boards with nails, and carried this like that-- it was heavy-- to the oven. And it bakes there. And then was ready, the pots and pans. It was in white and other colors. Yes, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you weren't working, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just talking, and walking, and eating, and just trying not to work too hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience hunger in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not that we been starving, starving to death, but was not enough. In this age, you need food [INAUDIBLE]. And even in his camp was better than in Plaszow or everywhere, but there was not so much food. Was not enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was night like in the barracks? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've been talking. And was so many people and everybody have different talents. For example, my one friend, she was a poet. She writes poems. And she was reading to us. And she was very intelligent, very skilled girls. And one was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frida, Frida. Last name, I don't remember. A long time ago, I know it, but I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: She wrote poetry inside the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. One, she was very talented for making beautiful gloves from leather. And they find out this, so they brought her leather and she make for the wife of Germans, she make the gloves. And everybody did something. But we, every day, we went to the factory to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long I work? Until the German, the Russian progress, and he is to liquidate the factory and move to Czechoslovakia. And he moved us with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you work in a factory before he had to move you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know it was summer, and spring, and summer. And then in November, then he move, they moved us.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in his factory, did you see anyone get murdered or beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody, no at Oskar Schindler. Never. No in Krakow. No in Brunnlitz. Nobody was beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you were on Schindler's List?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was his people. And not only this, he say to the German, you will not touch them. They are my people. He has guts, I tell you. He spent money on us. He was good to us. He was a great man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know he spent money on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know. We know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he spend money on you for?

speaker: interviewee
text: To bribe the Germans so they let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that. Where were you when they let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We when for example, we been in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he has to liquidate. The Russian progress, so he has to liquidate. And somebody say it was by mistake what I think. We have to stop in Auschwitz, whole transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he come to tell you he had to? How did he had to liquidate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew the Russian coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I from outside it cames. From mouth to mouth, it came the news that the Russian progress. So we knew. And then they move us. So we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they move you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us on train and we went. It was a terrible feeling to come to Auschwitz, the chimneys, the smell of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what Auschwitz was before you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew, we knew. We knew in Ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a terrible smell, hair and bodies. You could smell. And was very scary. And we didn't know how long we were in there, whether one time we will go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived, what did they do to you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put us in shower. And we didn't know what a shower is, just because we heard that gas they put in a shower. But we really been the protection there from Schindler. And he not let us go to the gas. They cut our hair not completely, just short, very short.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, you were protected by Schindler in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was behind us. He said, this is my group. They mark my people. They good workers. So probably, this where he need the money to bribe them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know he was behind you when you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just we heard, we heard. And we have a good feeling that he is with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did they treat you, since you belonged to Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't treat us bad. For example, they did not shave the hair, just leave short. It will make a difference for girls. And but I remember one time when, was when I was working at the laundry place, he came and he started to talk to us. And he say a very bad time is coming. I don't know what we will do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Morlock. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: --you went to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before we went to Auschwitz. This was in the wash machine, in the wash place, laundry place in Plaszow. And he said the big [? Morlock ?] is coming. This what he says. I still remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: This happened before you were at the barracks for Schindler? You were still--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was still in the concentration camp on the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Schindler came to speak to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we been working. We been working there. And when we belong to him, then he came to us, to the group. And he say, the big [? Morlock ?] is coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Morlock? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Morlock, ?] like the killer. And he was worried. He was worried about us. And this what I think, he did everything, spent his money, and bribe them, and save us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before you went to the barracks at his factory or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before. Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to his factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then we went to him. But we stop in Auschwitz

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he had to liquidate the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to move the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went where? You landed in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then went to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did he get you out of Auschwitz? How did you leave Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: He bribed them, probably he bribed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know all the details, you know. We didn't have newspaper. We didn't have radio. We didn't know nothing, just what you could hear from people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: One month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the ovens and you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and it was terrible feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet anyone you knew from your village while you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, I met there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that this Madrit group is there. So I was asking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Madrit, where she was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-D-R-I-T, Madrit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the in Spain, this capital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in Spain, Madrid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was this, where she was working. But he didn't have this power like Oskar Schindler to save them, so they went to Auschwitz. And from Auschwitz, they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw your sister in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I saw my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What condition was she in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw my father in Auschwitz too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, where did you see him in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the man been. So I been asking, do you see [? Lukower? ?] And they say, no, go there, go there. And I found him. And I found my sister too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they doing? When you found your father, what was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, just sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you say to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We were happy to see each other, but we didn't know the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: What condition was he in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was good looking. He was not skinny, like starving or something. And I say, oh, you look good. I was happy how he looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. This was not work to do there. We didn't do nothing, just waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your sister do in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: No work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No work. This is worse than to work, you know that? The day is so long and so boring. This was another punishment, not to work. And the uppers were there also in morning, early in morning get up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The upper, what I say, they wake us up 5:00. And we have to go out, everybody. And they start to count us.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you met your sister, what did you say to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: What we could say, we didn't know what will be. But she say, I have a good feeling that you at Schindler, she say. So the people say that compared to this, what we are in Auschwitz is like a sanatorium. She, this is what she says to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time you saw your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the last time. This was the last time in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they, when the Russian progress, they have to liquidate. They put them on the tracks, took to the Baltic ocean, and sunk them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sunk them in the Baltic ocean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she in? How did they sink her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how. Probably, on a boat, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. After the war, I was asking and asking. I went to the Jewish Community Center in Krakow every day. I went there every day, ask about. About my mother I knew. But I didn't know about my sister and father. So I find--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your father perish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you that he has the carbuncle here and got the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What what there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carbuncle it's called. It is growth but is yellow from puss. It's infection. And so they give him the shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how he got the shot? What did, he had the infection and what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, just I heard. I don't know the details.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he got the infection. And instead to heal this what was not a problem, it was not penicillin this time, but still they could help him.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no penicillin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not during the war. And they give him a shot, a poison shot and he passed away. This what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Oskar Schindler came, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And got you out of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that? Did you see him coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just know that came the trucks. They put us on the train. It was November, was snowing. And with the train, we went to Brinice, to this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brinice it's called this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-I-N-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of place was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Czechoslovakia, Moravia part from Czechoslovakia. There he established another factory. But not the enamel factory, but arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he worked on arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to work again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this factory different from the last one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was completely different-- different machines and different work. But what we heard that the arms was not good. What he did didn't work. They didn't work. But he, they didn't blame him. They didn't kill him for that. But this what we heard.
